title: Altman and Amodei walk back the AI jobs apocalypse — while 50,000 people lose jobs to AI in 2026
subtitle: Two CEOs who spent 2025 warning that white-collar work was ending now say they were wrong. The layoff data tells a more complicated story.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman told a Commonwealth Bank of Australia conference in recent weeks that he was 'pretty wrong' about AI's impact on entry-level white-collar work. 'I'm delighted to be wrong about this,' he added. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei — who a year ago predicted AI could eliminate up to 50% of white-collar jobs — has since suggested automation may expand the scope of human work rather than replace it. Both reversals come as OpenAI moves through its confidential IPO filing at an estimated valuation north of $1 trillion, a process for which apocalyptic labor rhetoric is a liability.

The data is more mixed than either walk-back suggests. Challenger, Gray & Christmas counts roughly 50,000 US job cuts attributed to AI in 2026 through mid-year — about 17% of the 300,000 total layoffs announced. Mercer's Global Talent Trends 2026 survey found 99% of C-suite respondents expect AI to drive at least some workforce reduction within two years. But a working paper from the National Bureau of Economic Research found fewer than half of CFOs plan AI-related cuts, which translates to about 0.4% of the US workforce this year — meaningful, but not the mass unemployment event Amodei once described.

The whiplash matters because CEO forecasts are load-bearing for both policy and investment. When Amodei warned of 50% white-collar displacement, it framed labor debates, shaped regulatory posture, and let executives justify preemptive cuts. Reversing the message now — with an IPO in flight and a political climate where 5% government stakes are on the table — reads less like new evidence and more like new incentives. The underlying question, whether AI is a labor-augmenting or labor-replacing technology at scale, has not been settled by anyone.

A takeaway for learners: watch the incentive structure behind every forecast, not just the forecast itself. The same executive can predict apocalypse and expansion within eighteen months without either being dishonest — the framing tracks what they need to communicate. For your own career, the useful signal is the raw data: 50,000 real layoffs, a 33% year-over-year acceleration, and 48% of tracked cuts explicitly blamed on automation. Plan for the world you can measure, not the one being sold on the earnings call.
